does: This paper conducts a systematic empirical evaluation of activation patching, a core mechanistic interpretability technique used to identify important model components in language models. The authors examine multiple methodological variants including different evaluation metrics and corruption methods for causal interventions. Through extensive experiments on localization and circuit discovery tasks, they demonstrate that seemingly minor hyperparameter choices—such as how to measure intervention effects or how to corrupt activations—can substantially alter interpretability findings. The paper moves beyond treating activation patching as a monolithic technique and identifies which design choices produce more reliable and informative results across different interpretability objectives.
connects: This work builds directly on the activation patching framework introduced by Vig et al. (2020) and extends the growing literature on mechanistic interpretability of large language models. It responds to the fragmentation in prior mechanistic interpretability work, where researchers have applied variants of causal intervention methods with inconsistent choices about metrics and procedures. The paper engages with foundational work on understanding model internals through component attribution and causal analysis, while also connecting to broader efforts in neural network interpretability that seek systematic best practices. By formalizing the choices embedded in prior applications of activation patching, it addresses a methodological gap that has emerged as mechanistic interpretability has matured into an active research area with multiple competing approaches.
enables: This work establishes clearer methodological foundations for future mechanistic interpretability research, allowing subsequent studies to make principled design choices rather than arbitrary ones. The identified best practices should increase reproducibility and comparability across mechanistic interpretability papers that rely on activation patching. By clarifying which metrics and corruption methods produce more reliable localization and circuit discovery results, the paper enables researchers to more confidently identify model components underlying specific behaviors. This standardization could accelerate mechanistic interpretability research by reducing methodological confusion and making findings more robust. The systematic analysis also opens avenues for understanding why certain intervention designs work better, potentially leading to novel intervention techniques or improved understanding of what activation patching can and cannot reveal about model mechanisms.
